original_abstract: Attention mechanisms have proven highly effective in enhancing feature representations for various computer vision tasks by modeling spatial or channel dependencies. However, many existing methods rely on channel dimensionality reduction to manage computational costs, which can inadvertently obscure deep visual semantics. In this paper, we introduce the Efficient Multi-Scale Attention (EMA) module, a novel framework designed to improve representational power while maintaining computational efficiency. The EMA module avoids dimensionality reduction by reshaping a portion of the channels into the batch dimension and partitioning channel dimensions into multiple sub-group features, ensuring that spatial semantic information is well-distributed within each group. To capture comprehensive dependencies, we employ large-scale parallel subnetworks that encode global information for channel re-calibration. These parallel branches are then integrated through a cross-spatial learning method, utilizing cross-dimension interaction to capture critical pixel-level pairwise relationships. Rigorous evaluation across multiple benchmarks—including CIFAR-100, ImageNet-1k, MS COCO, and VisDrone2019—demonstrates that EMA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art attention mechanisms in image classification and object detection tasks while requiring fewer parameters and lower computational overhead.
humanized_abstract: Attention mechanisms usually achieve significant improvements in the feature representations by modeling the spatial or channel relationships. However, the channel dimension reduction of existing attention models may overlook some deep visual semantics, which leads to performance degradation. In this work, we propose an Efficient Multi-Scale Attention (EMA) module, which not only improves the representation capacity but also maintains the efficiency. Unlike previous attention models that reduce the channel dimension, the proposed EMA module maintains the original channel number by reshaping the half channels into the batch dimension and grouping the rest channels into multiple sub-group features with evenly distributed spatial semantic information. To capture the multi-scale dependencies of the channel features, the EMA module adopts large-scale parallel subnetworks to obtain the global channel calibration features. The outputs of the parallel subnetworks are then merged by cross-spatial learning that utilizes the cross-dimension interaction to model the essential pixel-level pairwise cross-dimension relationships among the group features. Experimental results show that the proposed EMA module achieves better performance than the state-of-the-art attention models on CIFAR-100, ImageNet-1k, MS COCO and VisDrone2019 datasets while with fewer parameters and lower computational cost in image classification and object detection tasks.
